description: This flow chart illustrates the typical laboratory-scale thermomechanical processing route for producing Fe-Al-(C) and Fe-Al-Mn-C ferritic and multiphase sheet steels. The process begins with ingot casting, followed by reheating at 1250°C and rough rolling. After a second reheating at 1250°C, hot rolling is performed to a final thickness of 3.5 mm with a finish rolling temperature (FRT) of 900°C, followed by run-out table cooling and coiling. The coiled strip then undergoes pickling at 85°C using HCl, followed by cold rolling or warm rolling at 250°C to a final thickness of 1 mm. Subsequently, continuous annealing is conducted at 850-900°C, with a final aging step at 400-700°C specifically for Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys. The diagram indicates that while these processing steps correspond to industrial routes used for conventional automotive steels, the thermomechanical parameters (temperature, cooling rate, rolling reduction) must be carefully selected based on alloy chemistry—particularly the coiling temperature, which ranges from high temperatures (~650°C) for ultralow C Fe-Al-(C) alloys to room temperature for high-alloy Fe-Mn-Al-C steels—to suppress brittle κ-carbide precipitation and ensure adequate formability.
